concepts.name: svm for regression
concepts.description: Extension of support vector machines to regression problems using epsilon-insensitive loss to create a tube around the prediction function.
concepts.existing_quote: This is an "ε-insensitive" error measure, ignoring errors of size less than ε.

paragraphs.text:
This is page 417
Printer: Opaque this
12
Support Vector Machines and
Flexible Discriminants

In this chapter we describe generalizations of linear decision boundaries
for classiﬁcation. Optimal separating hyperplanes are introduced in Chap-
ter 4 for the case when two classes are linearly separable. Here we cover
extensions to the nonseparable case, where the classes overlap. These tech-
niques are then generalized to what is known as the support vector machine,
which produces nonlinear boundaries by constructing a linear boundary in
a large, transformed version of the feature space. The second set of methods
generalize Fisher’s linear discriminant analysis (LDA). The generalizations
include ﬂexible discriminant analysis which facilitates construction of non-
linear boundaries in a manner very similar to the support vector machines,
penalized discriminant analysis for problems such as signal and image clas-
siﬁcation where the large number of features are highly correlated, and
mixture discriminant analysis for irregularly shaped classes.

In Chapter 4 we discussed a technique for constructing an optimal separat-
ing hyperplane between two perfectly separated classes. We review this and
generalize to the nonseparable case, where the classes may not be separable
by a linear boundary.

concepts.name: smoothing splines
concepts.description: A regression method using spline basis functions with penalty on roughness, related to projection pursuit and regularization methods.
concepts.existing_quote: Roosen and Hastie (1994) present a formulation using smoothing splines.

This is page 417
Printer: Opaque this
12

concepts.name: radial basis function expansion
concepts.description: A basis expansion method using radial basis functions, mathematically equivalent to certain kernel methods and regularization estimates.
concepts.existing_quote: Notice that this has the same form as both the radial basis function expansion and a regularization estimate, discussed in Chapters 5 and 6.

concepts.name: regularization estimate
concepts.description: A statistical estimation method that adds a penalty term to prevent overfitting, mathematically connected to kernel methods and basis expansions.
concepts.existing_quote: Notice that this has the same form as both the radial basis function expansion and a regularization estimate, discussed in Chapters 5 and 6.

concepts.name: training-class separation
concepts.description: The degree to which classes can be separated in a feature space, improved by enlarging the space with basis expansions.
concepts.existing_quote: Generally linear boundaries in the enlarged space achieve better training-class separation

concepts.name: kkt conditions
concepts.description: Karush-Kuhn-Tucker optimality conditions that characterize the solution to constrained optimization problems, implying labeled points fall into distinct groups.
concepts.existing_quote: It can be shown that the KKT optimality conditions imply that the labeled points (xi, yi) fall into three distinct groups

concepts.name: linear inequality constraints
concepts.description: Constraints of the form g(x) ≤ 0 in optimization problems, which preserve convexity when combined with a convex objective.

concepts.description:
with nonzero coeﬃcients ˆαi only for those observations i for which the
constraints in (12.16) are exactly met (due to (12.14)). These observations
are called the support vectors, since ˆβ is represented in terms of them
alone. Among these support points, some will lie on the edge of the margin
(ˆξi = 0), and hence from (12.15) and (12.12) will be characterized by
0 < ˆαi < C; the remainder (ˆξi > 0) have ˆαi = C. From (12.14) we can
see that any of these margin points (0 < ˆαi, ˆξi = 0) can be used to solve
for β0, and we typically use an average of all the solutions for numerical
stability.
Maximizing the dual (12.13) is a simpler convex quadratic programming
problem than the primal (12.9), and can be solved with standard techniques
(Murray et al., 1981, for example).

concepts.name: linear decision boundary
concepts.description: A hyperplane that separates classes in the original feature space, defined by f(x) = x^T β + β0 = 0.
concepts.existing_quote: In this chapter we describe generalizations of linear decision boundaries for classification.

concepts.name: signed distance
concepts.description: The distance from a point x to a hyperplane, with sign indicating which side of the boundary, given by f(x) = x^T β + β0.
concepts.existing_quote: f(x) in (12.1) gives the signed distance from a point x to the hyperplane f(x) = xT β+β0 = 0

concepts.name: tuning parameter
concepts.description: A parameter (such as C in SVMs) that controls model complexity and must be selected via methods like cross-validation.
concepts.existing_quote: The tuning parameter of this procedure is the cost parameter C.

paragraphs.text: From (12.10) we see that the solution for β has the form

with nonzero coeﬃcients ˆαi only for those observations i for which the
constraints in (12.16) are exactly met (due to (12.14)). These observations
are called the support vectors, since ˆβ is represented in terms of them
alone. Among these support points, some will lie on the edge of the margin
(ˆξi = 0), and hence from (12.15) and (12.12) will be characterized by
0 < ˆαi < C; the remainder (ˆξi > 0) have ˆαi = C. From (12.14) we can
see that any of these margin points (0 < ˆαi, ˆξi = 0) can be used to solve
for β0, and we typically use an average of all the solutions for numerical
stability.
Maximizing the dual (12.13) is a simpler convex quadratic programming
problem than the primal (12.9), and can be solved with standard techniques
(Murray et al., 1981, for example).
Given the solutions ˆβ0 and ˆβ, the decision function can be written as
The tuning parameter of this procedure is the cost parameter C.

concepts.name: kernel function
concepts.description: A function K(x, x') that computes inner products in a transformed feature space without explicitly computing the transformation h(x).
concepts.existing_quote: we need not specify the transformation h(x) at all, but require only knowledge of the kernel function

paragraphs.text:
The support vector classiﬁer described so far ﬁnds linear boundaries in the
input feature space. As with other linear methods, we can make the pro-
cedure more ﬂexible by enlarging the feature space using basis expansions
such as polynomials or splines (Chapter 5). Generally linear boundaries
in the enlarged space achieve better training-class separation, and trans-
late to nonlinear boundaries in the original space. Once the basis functions
hm(x), m = 1, . . . , M are selected, the procedure is the same as before. We
ﬁt the SV classiﬁer using input features h(xi) = (h1(xi), h2(xi), . . . , hM(xi)),
i = 1, . . . , N, and produce the (nonlinear) function ˆf(x) = h(x)T ˆβ + ˆβ0.
The classiﬁer is ˆG(x) = sign( ˆf(x)) as before.
The support vector machine classiﬁer is an extension of this idea, where
the dimension of the enlarged space is allowed to get very large, inﬁnite
in some cases. It might seem that the computations would become pro-
hibitive. It would also seem that with suﬃcient basis functions, the data
would be separable, and overﬁtting would occur. We ﬁrst show how the
SVM technology deals with these issues. We then see that in fact the SVM
classiﬁer is solving a function-ﬁtting problem using a particular criterion
and form of regularization, and is part of a much bigger class of problems
that includes the smoothing splines of Chapter 5. The reader may wish
to consult Section 5.8, which provides background material and overlaps
somewhat with the next two sections.

We can represent the optimization problem (12.9) and its solution in a
special way that only involves the input features via inner products. We do
this directly for the transformed feature vectors h(xi). We then see that for
particular choices of h, these inner products can be computed very cheaply.
The Lagrange dual function (12.13) has the form
From (12.10) we see that the solution function f(x) can be written

As before, given αi, β0 can be determined by solving yif(xi) = 1 in (12.20)
for any (or all) xi for which 0 < αi < C.

So both (12.19) and (12.20) involve h(x) only through inner products. In
fact, we need not specify the transformation h(x) at all, but require only
knowledge of the kernel function

that computes inner products in the transformed space. K should be a
symmetric positive (semi-) deﬁnite function; see Section 5.8.1.
Three popular choices for K in the SVM literature are

Radial basis: K(x, x′) = exp(−γ∥x −x′∥2),
Neural network: K(x, x′) = tanh(κ1⟨x, x′⟩+ κ2).

Consider for example a feature space with two inputs X1 and X2, and a
polynomial kernel of degree 2. Then

Then M = 6, and if we choose h1(X) = 1, h2(X) =
√

concepts.name: polynomial kernel
concepts.description: A kernel function of the form K(x, x') = (1 + ⟨x, x'⟩)^d that computes polynomial feature expansions efficiently.
concepts.existing_quote: Consider for example a feature space with two inputs X1 and X2, and a polynomial kernel of degree 2.

concepts.name: radial basis kernel
concepts.description: A kernel function K(x, x') = exp(-γ||x - x'||²) that measures similarity based on Euclidean distance, also known as RBF or Gaussian kernel.
concepts.existing_quote: Radial basis: K(x, x′) = exp(−γ∥x −x′∥²)

paragraphs.text:
The support vector classiﬁer described so far ﬁnds linear boundaries in the
input feature space. As with other linear methods, we can make the pro-
cedure more ﬂexible by enlarging the feature space using basis expansions
such as polynomials or splines (Chapter 5). Generally linear boundaries
in the enlarged space achieve better training-class separation, and trans-
late to nonlinear boundaries in the original space. Once the basis functions
hm(x), m = 1, . . . , M are selected, the procedure is the same as before. We
ﬁt the SV classiﬁer using input features h(xi) = (h1(xi), h2(xi), . . . , hM(xi)),
i = 1, . . . , N, and produce the (nonlinear) function ˆf(x) = h(x)T ˆβ + ˆβ0.
The classiﬁer is ˆG(x) = sign( ˆf(x)) as before.
The support vector machine classiﬁer is an extension of this idea, where
the dimension of the enlarged space is allowed to get very large, inﬁnite
in some cases. It might seem that the computations would become pro-
hibitive. It would also seem that with suﬃcient basis functions, the data
would be separable, and overﬁtting would occur. We ﬁrst show how the
SVM technology deals with these issues. We then see that in fact the SVM
classiﬁer is solving a function-ﬁtting problem using a particular criterion
and form of regularization, and is part of a much bigger class of problems
that includes the smoothing splines of Chapter 5. The reader may wish
to consult Section 5.8, which provides background material and overlaps
somewhat with the next two sections.

We can represent the optimization problem (12.9) and its solution in a
special way that only involves the input features via inner products. We do
this directly for the transformed feature vectors h(xi). We then see that for
particular choices of h, these inner products can be computed very cheaply.
The Lagrange dual function (12.13) has the form
From (12.10) we see that the solution function f(x) can be written

As before, given αi, β0 can be determined by solving yif(xi) = 1 in (12.20)
for any (or all) xi for which 0 < αi < C.

So both (12.19) and (12.20) involve h(x) only through inner products. In
fact, we need not specify the transformation h(x) at all, but require only
knowledge of the kernel function

that computes inner products in the transformed space. K should be a
symmetric positive (semi-) deﬁnite function; see Section 5.8.1.
Three popular choices for K in the SVM literature are

Radial basis: K(x, x′) = exp(−γ∥x −x′∥2),
Neural network: K(x, x′) = tanh(κ1⟨x, x′⟩+ κ2).

Consider for example a feature space with two inputs X1 and X2, and a
polynomial kernel of degree 2. Then

Then M = 6, and if we choose h1(X) = 1, h2(X) =
√

concepts.name: neural network kernel
concepts.description: A kernel function K(x, x') = tanh(κ1⟨x, x'⟩ + κ2) inspired by neural network activation functions.
concepts.existing_quote: Neural network: K(x, x′) = tanh(κ1⟨x, x′⟩+ κ2).

paragraphs.text:
The support vector classiﬁer described so far ﬁnds linear boundaries in the
input feature space. As with other linear methods, we can make the pro-
cedure more ﬂexible by enlarging the feature space using basis expansions
such as polynomials or splines (Chapter 5). Generally linear boundaries
in the enlarged space achieve better training-class separation, and trans-
late to nonlinear boundaries in the original space. Once the basis functions
hm(x), m = 1, . . . , M are selected, the procedure is the same as before. We
ﬁt the SV classiﬁer using input features h(xi) = (h1(xi), h2(xi), . . . , hM(xi)),
i = 1, . . . , N, and produce the (nonlinear) function ˆf(x) = h(x)T ˆβ + ˆβ0.
The classiﬁer is ˆG(x) = sign( ˆf(x)) as before.
The support vector machine classiﬁer is an extension of this idea, where
the dimension of the enlarged space is allowed to get very large, inﬁnite
in some cases. It might seem that the computations would become pro-
hibitive. It would also seem that with suﬃcient basis functions, the data
would be separable, and overﬁtting would occur. We ﬁrst show how the
SVM technology deals with these issues. We then see that in fact the SVM

and form of regularization, and is part of a much bigger class of problems
